planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; seeds cold-stratify naturally and germinate with winter rains for spring bloom.

directions: Punchbowl Clarkia is a charming southern California and Baja California native annual with slender, branching stems bearing small, 4-petaled, rose-pink to lavender-pink flowers with darker veins along dry, rocky, partially shaded chaparral and canyon slopes. It is one of the few Clarkia species that tolerates and even prefers partial shade, making it particularly valuable under native oaks and along shaded garden borders. Direct sow in fall on dry, lean, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun, pressing seeds lightly into the surface without covering — like all Clarkia, it does not transplant well and must be direct sown. Once established it self-sows in suitable conditions and is an excellent shade-tolerant native annual for California dry gardens and open chaparral garden settings.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; one of the most cold-tolerant Clarkia species, seeds overwinter reliably across most of its range and germinate in spring.

directions: Diamond Clarkia is a widespread western US native annual with slender, upright stems and small, 4-petaled flowers in rose-lavender to light purple whose distinctively rhombus- or diamond-shaped petals give the species both its common and scientific names. It is native to dry, open foothill grasslands, oak woodlands, and chaparral margins from British Columbia south to Baja California and east to Utah, making it one of the most broadly distributed and cold-tolerant Clarkia species in the west. Direct sow in fall on dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun — it thrives in poor soils and declines in rich, amended ground. It self-sows freely in open, disturbed ground and is an excellent component of native wildflower seed mixes for western US meadow plantings and restoration projects.

planting.note: Direct sow on bare, dry, well-drained, lean soil in fall; seeds cold-stratify over winter and germinate in early spring for late spring bloom.

directions: Slender Clarkia is a graceful California and Oregon native annual with wiry, branching stems and pretty, 4-petaled flowers in shades of lavender-pink to rose, each petal bearing a contrasting darker inner zone that draws specialist native bees. It is native to dry foothill grasslands, chaparral margins, and open woodland clearings throughout the California Coast Ranges, Sierra Nevada foothills, and southern Oregon. Direct sow on bare, dry, lean, well-drained soil in fall — it resents transplanting and performs best with no soil amendment, as it is adapted to the poor, rocky soils of its native habitat. It self-sows freely in suitable conditions and pairs beautifully with California poppies and annual phlox for a naturalistic spring wildflower display in dry California gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring after last frost; cold stratification of seed for 4-6 weeks improves germination if starting from seed.

directions: Oregon Checker-mallow is a robust native perennial of moist meadows, wet prairies, and stream margins from Washington to northern California, producing tall, upright spikes of bright pink to rose-pink, 5-petaled mallow flowers 1 to 1.5 inches across from June through August. It is one of the showiest and most widely cultivated western Sidalcea species, performing best in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade. Plant container-grown transplants in spring, spacing generously to allow mature clumps to develop over several seasons; established plants are long-lived and drought-tolerant once roots reach moist subsoil. It is an excellent choice for Pacific Northwest native gardens, rain gardens, and perennial borders, where it serves as a larval host for West Coast lady butterflies and draws specialist native bees to its nectar-rich blooms all summer.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in consistently moist to wet soil; keep soil evenly moist through the first growing season to establish deep roots.

directions: Henderson's Checker-mallow is a rare native Sidalcea of brackish marsh margins, tidal meadows, and wet coastal lowland habitats in the Pacific Northwest, primarily in Washington and Oregon, where it produces erect spikes of deep rose-pink mallow flowers in summer atop sturdy, upright stems. It is one of the most moisture-tolerant checker-mallows and thrives in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, mucky or heavy soil in full sun — conditions that would be fatal to most ornamental perennials. Plant container-grown transplants in spring and keep soil wet through establishment; once rooted, plants form long-lived clumps that spread slowly by rhizomes and naturalize well in restored wetland settings. It is an important larval host for specialist pollinators in its native range and an excellent choice for rain gardens, wet borders, and Pacific Northwest wetland restoration plantings.
